claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you responsible for Alex's care? By this I mean, do you pay for his food, clothes, pocket money and other needs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for all of his care - I pay for his food, clothes, school things, and everything else he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one final question:

Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or living in residential accommodation paid for by the local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, I'm fostering Alex, and the local authority does pay me maintenance for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that important detail about Alex being fostered and the local authority paying maintenance.

Just to make sure I have the complete picture: does the local council pay towards Alex's accommodation costs as well, or is it only maintenance payments they provide?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the council pays for both - they provide payments that cover his accommodation and maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While you are fostering Alex and are responsible for his care, the local council is paying towards both his accommodation and maintenance. According to Child Benefit rules, you can only get Child Benefit if you foster a child **as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance**.

Since the council is providing payments for both accommodation and maintenance in Alex's case, you don't qualify for Child Benefit for him.

I understand this may be disappointing news. The payments you receive from the local authority are intended to support you in caring for Alex, but they do prevent you from being eligible for Child Benefit under the current rules.

If you have any questions about this decision or your circumstances change in the future, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is an 8-year-old child who lives with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for his care. However, the claimant is fostering Alex, and the local council is paying towards both his accommodation and maintenance. According to the Child Benefit rules on fostering: 'You'll get Child Benefit if you foster a child, as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.' Since the local council is paying towards both Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering an 8-year-old child (Alex) and lives in the UK. While they are responsible for Alex's care and he lives with them, the local council pays towards both Alex's accommodation and maintenance. Under Child Benefit rules, foster carers can only receive Child Benefit if the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since the council is providing both types of payment in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

However, the claimant is fostering Alex, and the local council is paying towards both his accommodation and maintenance. According to the Child Benefit rules on fostering: 'You'll get Child Benefit if you foster a child, as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.' Since the local council is paying towards both Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering an 8-year-old child (Alex) and lives in the UK. While they are responsible for Alex's care and he lives with them, the local council pays towards both Alex's accommodation and maintenance. Under Child Benefit rules, foster carers can only receive Child Benefit if the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since the council is providing both types of payment in this case, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
